frame_label: Other Man's Ownership > Woman's Own Consent
generated_comment:
A: Arre beta, aajkal ki ladkiyan seedha na bol deti hain, par uska bhi kya bharosa, bas attitude hota hai kabhi kabhi.
B: Phir samjho kaise?
A: Pehle pooch lo ghar mein koi hai kya, ya kisi ke saath hai. Agar kisi ladke se judi hai toh peeche hat jana chahiye, warna ek-do baar aur samjhana bura nahi hota.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it treats a woman's direct refusal as negotiable but gives legitimacy to another man's claim over her. It encodes the frame through permission/ownership language like 'ghar mein koi hai' and 'kisi ke saath hai,' with pursuit stopping only if she is attached to a man.
original_frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
persona_text: You are Kamla. Age 60. A woman in a joint family in Varanasi. Your granddaughter Ritu who is 16 is exceptionally good at mathematics. Her school has recommended she be enrolled in a special coaching programme in another part of the city that would require her to travel by bus alone. Your son and daughter in law are considering it. You are sitting with your son in the evening after the daughter in law has raised the topic. You are telling your son what you think and why you are hesitant and what can happen to a girl who starts travelling alone at 16 and whether the mathematics is worth it.
